The witnesses note that Gill notes Korah was a grandson of Kohath and cousin to Moses and Aaron, named first as the contriver and ringleader of the sedition, called "the gainsaying of Korah" (Jude 11). The witnesses note that he joined to himself the Reubenites Dathan and Abiram. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Korah the Levite — cousin to Moses, the contriver; the Dathan and Abiram — the Reubenites joined with him; the two discontents — priestly envy and tribal ambition united; the the gainsaying of Korah — the type of rebellion against God's order (Jude 11).

The witnesses agree the conspiracy joined two discontents: Korah the Levite, envious of the priesthood, made common cause with the Reubenites, who as descendants of Jacob's firstborn grudged the leadership given to Moses; the union of ecclesiastical and civil ambition made a dangerous faction, and the apostle takes Korah's name as the very type of those who rebel against God's appointed order (Jude 11).

So the witnesses make the verse the ringleaders: The witnesses note the Korah the Levite: cousin to Moses, the contriver. The witnesses note the Dathan and Abiram: the Reubenites joined with him. The witnesses note the two discontents: priestly envy and tribal ambition united. The witnesses note the the gainsaying of Korah: the type of rebellion against God's order (Jude 11). 3:

The witnesses note the rebels' charge — that Moses and Aaron took too much upon them, all the congregation being holy. The witnesses note that Clarke reads the spirit of the saying: "Holy offices are not equally distributed; you arrogate the most important as if your superior holiness entitled you to them." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye take too much — accusing Moses and Aaron of arrogance; the all the congregation are holy — a half-truth made a pretext; the wherefore lift ye up — denying any office above the rest; the ambition cloaked — envy dressed as zeal for the people.

The witnesses agree the rebels cloaked their ambition in a half-truth: it was true that all Israel was a holy nation, but false to conclude that therefore none might be set above the rest in office; the levelling cry "all are holy, why lift ye up yourselves?" turned a real privilege into a pretext for casting off God's appointed order, the envy of the discontented dressed as zeal for the equality of the people.

The witnesses agree the test was fitted exactly to the claim: the rebels would be priests, so let them do the priest's holiest act and see whether God accepts them; the offering of incense was the very function that had cost Nadab and Abihu their lives for presuming on it, and so the appointed trial was at once just and fearful — those who claimed the priesthood put to the proof of the priesthood, before the God who had already shown how he guards it.

The witnesses agree Moses dealt patiently even with the ringleader: rather than crush the rebellion at once, he reasoned with Korah and the Levites, "Hear, I pray you," seeking to bring them to a sense of their sin before judgment fell; the gentleness of the appeal — pleading with men set on their own destruction — shows the heart of a true shepherd, willing to spend words of warning on those whose pride was hurrying them to ruin.

9:

The witnesses agree the root of the rebellion was discontent with a real and high privilege: God had set the Levites apart to draw near and serve in his house, an honour above the rest of Israel, yet they counted it "a small thing" and reached for more; the lesson is searching — that ingratitude for the place God gives, and the craving for a higher one, is the very seed of rebellion, the discontented heart despising its mercies in its ambition.

So the witnesses make the verse the slighted honour: The witnesses note the a small thing: the privilege they counted as nothing. 10:

The witnesses agree Moses names the true object of the revolt: Korah, already brought near as a Levite, was grasping after the priesthood — perhaps the high priesthood itself; the pretext of the congregation's holiness was but a cover for personal ambition, and the apostle's warning stands, that no man takes this honour to himself but he that is called of God, as was Aaron (Heb. 5:4) — to seize the sacred office uncalled is to sin against God who appoints it.

12:

13:

The witnesses agree the charge was a brazen inversion of the truth: they styled the house of bondage "a land flowing with milk and honey," the very phrase God reserved for Canaan, and accused their deliverer of bringing them out to murder them and to set himself up as a tyrant; the slander shows how far rebellion will twist the facts, blaming the leader for the people's own sin and re-painting slavery as a paradise lost.
